title: EIF4A

infobox.value: (**eIF4A**) family consists of 3 closely related [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") [EIF4A1](/wiki/EIF4A1 "EIF4A1"), [EIF4A2](/wiki/EIF4A2 "EIF4A2"), and [EIF4A3](/wiki/EIF4A3 "EIF4A3"). These factors are required for the binding of [mRNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA") to [40S](/wiki/40S "40S") [ribosomal](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") subunits. In addition these proteins are [helicases](/wiki/Helicase "Helicase") that function to unwind double-stranded [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA").[[1]](#cite_note-pmid12206455-1)[[2]](#cite_note-pmid18606994-2)

first_paragraph: The **eukaryotic initiation factor-4A** (**eIF4A**) family consists of 3 closely related [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") [EIF4A1](/wiki/EIF4A1 "EIF4A1"), [EIF4A2](/wiki/EIF4A2 "EIF4A2"), and [EIF4A3](/wiki/EIF4A3 "EIF4A3"). These factors are required for the binding of [mRNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA") to [40S](/wiki/40S "40S") [ribosomal](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") subunits. In addition these proteins are [helicases](/wiki/Helicase "Helicase") that function to unwind double-stranded [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA").[[1]](#cite_note-pmid12206455-1)[[2]](#cite_note-pmid18606994-2)

The **eukaryotic initiation factor-4A** (**eIF4A**) family consists of 3 closely related [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") [EIF4A1](/wiki/EIF4A1 "EIF4A1"), [EIF4A2](/wiki/EIF4A2 "EIF4A2"), and [EIF4A3](/wiki/EIF4A3 "EIF4A3"). These factors are required for the binding of [mRNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA") to [40S](/wiki/40S "40S") [ribosomal](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") subunits. In addition these proteins are [helicases](/wiki/Helicase "Helicase") that function to unwind double-stranded [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA").[[1]](#cite_note-pmid12206455-1)[[2]](#cite_note-pmid18606994-2)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4A&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

The mechanisms governing the basic subsistence of [eukaryotic cells](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") are immensely complex; it is therefore unsurprising that regulation occurs at a number of stages of [protein synthesis](/wiki/Protein_synthesis "Protein synthesis") – the regulation of translation has become a well-studied field.[[3]](#cite_note-gingras1999-3) Human translational control is of increasing research interest as it has connotations in a range of diseases.[[4]](#cite_note-Hollams_2002-4) Orthologs of many of the factors involved in human translation are shared by a range of eukaryotic organisms; some of which are used as [model systems](/wiki/Model_organism "Model organism") for the investigation of translation initiation and elongation, for example: [sea urchin](/wiki/Sea_urchin "Sea urchin") eggs upon fertilization,[[5]](#cite_note-Castaneda_1969-5) rodent brain[[6]](#cite_note-Vargas_1983-6) and rabbit reticulocytes.[[7]](#cite_note-li2001-7) Monod and Jacob were among the first to propose that "the synthesis of individual proteins may be provoked or suppressed within a cell, under the influence of specific external agents, and the relative rates at which different proteins may be profoundly altered, depending upon external conditions".[[8]](#cite_note-pmid13718526-8) Almost half a century after the flurry of postulations arising from the revelation of the central dogma of [molecular biology](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology"), of which the preceding supposition by Monod and Jacob is an example; contemporary researchers still have much to learn about the modulation of genetic expression. Synthesis of protein from [mature messenger RNA](/wiki/Mature_messenger_RNA "Mature messenger RNA") in eukaryotes is divided into translation initiation, elongation, and termination of these stages; the initiation of translation is the rate limiting step. Within the process of translation initiation; the bottleneck occurs shortly before the [ribosome](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") binds to the 5’ m7GTP facilitated by a number of proteins; it is at this stage that constrictions born of stress, [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") starvation etc. take effect.

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4A&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Function")]

Eukaryotic initiation factor complex 2 (eIF2) forms a [ternary complex](/wiki/Ternary_complex "Ternary complex") with [GTP](/wiki/Guanosine_triphosphate "Guanosine triphosphate") and the initiator [Met](/wiki/Methionine "Methionine")-[tRNA](/wiki/TRNA "TRNA") – this process is regulated by guanine nucleotide exchange and [phosphorylation](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation") and serves as the main [regulatory element](/wiki/Regulatory_sequence "Regulatory sequence") of the bottleneck of [gene expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression"). Before translation can progress to the elongation stage, a number of initiation factors must facilitate the synergy of the ribosome and the mRNA and ensure that the 5’ UTR of the mRNA is sufficiently devoid of [secondary structure](/wiki/Secondary_structure "Secondary structure"). Binding in this way is facilitated by group 4 eukaryotic initiation factors; [eIF4F](/wiki/EIF4F "EIF4F") has implications in the normal regulation of translation as well as the transformation and progression of cancerous cells; as such, it represents an interesting field of research.

## Mechanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4A&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mechanism")]

The repertoire of compounds involved in eukaryotic translation consists of initiation factor classes 1 – 6;[[9]](#cite_note-isbn0-87969-568-4-9) [eIF4F](/wiki/EIF4F "EIF4F") is responsible for the binding of capped mRNA to the [40S](/wiki/40S "40S") ribosomal subunit via [eIF3](/wiki/EIF3 "EIF3"). The mRNA cap is bound by [eIF4E](/wiki/EIF4E "EIF4E") (25 kDa), eIF4G (185 kDa) acts as a scaffold for the complex whilst the ATP-dependent RNA helicase eIF4A (46 kDa) processes the secondary structure of the mRNA 5’ UTR to render it more conducive to ribosomal binding and subsequent translation.[[10]](#cite_note-pmid9187654-10) Together these three proteins are referred to as [eIF4F](/wiki/EIF4F "EIF4F"). For maximal activity; eIF4A also requires [eIF4B](/wiki/EIF4B "EIF4B") (80 kDa), which itself is enhanced by [eIF4H](/wiki/EIF4H "EIF4H") (25 kDa).[[11]](#cite_note-pmid15528191-11) A study conducted by Bi *et al.* in [wheat germ](/wiki/Cereal_germ "Cereal germ") seemed to indicate that eIF4A has a higher binding affinity for ADP than ATP except in the presence of eIF4B, which increased the ATP binding affinity tenfold without affecting ADP affinity.[[12]](#cite_note-pmid10801326-12) Once bound to the 5’ cap of mRNA, this 48S complex then searches for the (usually) AUG [start codon](/wiki/Start_codon "Start codon") and translation begins.

## Genes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4A&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Genes")]

In humans, the gene encoding [eIF4A isoform I](/wiki/EIF4A1 "EIF4A1") has a transcript length of 1741bp, contains 11 exons, and is located on chromosome 17.[[13]](#cite_note-pmid8493113-13)[[14]](#cite_note-pmid9790779-14) The genes for human isoforms [II](/wiki/EIF4A2 "EIF4A2") and [III](/wiki/EIF4A3 "EIF4A3") reside on chromosomes 3[[15]](#cite_note-pmid8521730-15) and 17[[16]](#cite_note-pmid10623621-16)[[17]](#cite_note-pmid14730019-17) respectively.

## Proteins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4A&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Proteins")]

The 407 residue,[[15]](#cite_note-pmid8521730-15) 46 kDa,[[18]](#cite_note-pmid10590115-18) protein eIF4A is the prototypical member of the [DEAD box](/wiki/DEAD_box "DEAD box") helicase family, so-called due to their conserved four-residue D-E-A-D sequence. This family of helicases is found in a range of prokaryotic and eukaryotic organisms including humans, wherein they catalyse a variety of processes including embryogenesis and [RNA splicing](/wiki/Splicing_(genetics) "Splicing (genetics)") as well as translation initiation.[[19]](#cite_note-pmid1378397-19) Crystallographic analysis of yeast eIF4A carried out by Carruthers *et al.* (2000)[[20]](#cite_note-pmid11087862-20) revealed that the molecule is approximately 80 Å in length and has a “dumbbell” shape where the proximal section represents an 11 residue (18 Å) linker postulated to confer a degree of flexibility and distension to the molecule in solution. eIF4A is an abundant cytoplasmic protein.[[21]](#cite_note-pmid18316401-21)

Three isoforms of eIF4A exist; I and II share 95% amino acid similarity and have been found simultaneously in rabbit reticulocyte [eIF4F](/wiki/EIF4F "EIF4F") in a ratio of 4:1, respectively.[[22]](#cite_note-pmid8449919-22) The third isoform; eIF4A III, which shares only 65% similarity to the other isoforms is believed to be a core component of the exon junction complex involved in pre-mRNA splicing.[[23]](#cite_note-pmid16030146-23)
